author: @geninji6117
text: If you attached the teng to a ac/DC conversion chip and that to a fish tank water pump you can cycle the water back to the top allowing you to use all of the water turbines power. You would have to use multiple fish tank pumps each at 3.5 v with .1253 something amps required. Then you would have free energy so long as you kept the water from freezing or evaporating. I'm making one using a low rpm water wheel.

author: @simona625
text: You could put a waterwheel and another 2 TENGs (on both sides or make the run up out of TENGs) in the run up to the turbine, that's 5 lots of power, in total, from one water source.....

author: @l0I0I0I0
text: Hey Luke?  Wonder if you combined two additional things if you could greatly improve upon it even more?  1.  If you use a round vortex.  2.  If instead of 2 inches deep, if it had a round pipe increasing it 33 foot vertically which is the weight of one atmosphere.   The hydrostatic pressure will creat an incredible pressure and torque on the turbine.  Should be capable of producing many amps rather than milliamps with a more powerful motor.

author: @RocketSteve
text: Reading the paper from the HK uni I saw they made a mistake on the voltage level, however, it has got me thinking and if 50W/m^2 is harvestable from rain, there should be no reason why you couldn't have a solar panel at the top of a stack, then TENG panels under it at opposite angles, and repeat over say a 2m height? After I build my ridge wind turbine I will look at building TENG panels for the shed. Having solar panels only in winter means my 12V system requires mains power top up :(

author: @ciphergrey6298
text: Couldn’t you essentially make the water perpetual, making it a perpetual energy device?

replies.author: @kennethbain4290
replies.text:
Not without violating some laws of Thermodynamics - entropy !
Besides, if all the energy goes into making the water go around again, what good is it doing you ? You are aiming to subtract energy.

replies.author: @ciphergrey6298
replies.text: @kennethbain4290 i was thinking more in terms of valve pressure switch and gravity fed water perpetuation. Along with minimal motor assist if needed.

author: @TheRainbowTrainRide
text: Pretty unrelated,  but I wondered if you guys checked out ' wind swept and interesting ' kite generator?  Was on BBC news a few months back. Thanks for all these awesome inventions and work you do

replies.author: @Barskor1
replies.text: That tech would work far better if they just used buoyancy on the kite rather than depending on the wind for all the lift. By that I mean just enough to keep the kits from falling from gravity and the weight of the cables.

replies.author: @TheRainbowTrainRide
replies.text: @Barskor1 the tech, for me, can work far better than these stationary wind turbines. I agree , so much room for improvement, However, the scalability of these kite turbines and the low cost to build surely should be a route we can all pursue in our research? The guy charges his EV with it 🤯

replies.author: @TheRainbowTrainRide
replies.text: But yeh regarding buoyancy, I think a hydrogen balloon of some sort would solve the gravity issue maybe?

replies.author: @Barskor1
replies.text: @TheRainbowTrainRide You can shape inflatables to be kites and hydrogen would be a good lift gas for this.

author: @marktunney5000

replies.author: @MyNameIsLukey
replies.text: It was to see if I could generate using the kinetic flow of water. I will build a teng into the raceway to see if the potential flow will yield an improvement or not. Thanks mate!😁

author: @drakinfire7205
text: Try layering your TENG ex. graphite foil,  abs glue, graphite, abs glue and repeat. And put them in parallel but most of the time i get better voltage if all of the negative electrodes are separate or positive but one set connect. The voltage seem to double but i only tested two layers so i cant say for certain.

author: @michaelkolanda8712
text: A more home sized scaled up system  would be the exact opposite setup with teng roof panels flowing into either an accumulator or straight into your gutters with one or more turbines in line. You could feasibly generate electricity nearly the whole way from your roof to the ground.

author: @hbdc
text: I appreciate the shoutout Luke! The reason I thought of this is I'm planning a solar build out after I finish another project in hopefully a month or two. So I thought to myself, what if the solar frame had an extended water collector in an attempt to fill the gap in energy production given weather changes. Depending on the height of the solar array maybe adding a tank to collect the stream to be used as a cooling source or a battery very similar to your experiment. Maybe even combine different turbine types and make runways that best suit their properties. As always, great video. You guys are awesome and if I can help support the channels in any way just say the word. Nothin but love guys :)

author: @mrpants8976
text: So, my understanding is the energy is less than or equal to the energy needed to bring the water up to the top of said turbine as all the potential power that could be created from any possible sources before it gets back down to ground level, but with rain allowing for water being lifted up and brought to the higher level without any input from us, it's possible to scavenge more power than we ourselves put in, but a large reservoir would be needed for sustained power generation
